summary: Gruber responds to a Byte article claiming Apple customers are foolish and gullible for buying supposedly average products. He argues that this dismissive attitude toward Apple buyers has persisted for years but becomes increasingly untenable as Apple's customer base grows. He draws a parallel to Microsoft's leadership, suggesting they may share the same delusion that Apple's success is merely marketing trickery. Gruber frames Apple-haters as the real dogmatic cultists, and compares Microsoft's denial to RIM's — companies convinced the market still works the way it used to.
key_points: The claim that Apple customers are duped by marketing has been made for over a decade, with only the specific complaints changing
As Apple's customer base keeps growing, the 'foolish customers' argument becomes harder to sustain
Some people who don't appreciate Apple's design philosophy project their preference as objective truth and pathologize those who disagree
Microsoft's insistence that tablets are PCs may stem from the same delusion — that Apple customers are simply brainwashed
Microsoft risks becoming RIM writ large: convinced the world hasn't changed
money_quotes: It's ham-fisted link-bait, yes, but let's go with it.
Apple's products do not appeal to everyone. In particular, they do not appeal to those who have no appreciation for Apple-style design.
Some of those people, like Mandzych, have decided that there is something wrong with the people who buy Apple products, not that there is something appealing and unique about Apple's products.
It's the Apple-haters who are beginning to look more and more like dogmatic cultists who have their heads in the sand.
The answer might be as simple as that they too believe — or are at least choosing to believe — that Apple customers are foolish and gullible, brainwashed by the Pied Piper of Apple's marketing magic.
Microsoft might be RIM writ large — convinced that things still are the way they were.
key_insight: Dismissing Apple's growing success as mass delusion is itself a form of denial, and companies like Microsoft risk becoming the next RIM by clinging to that belief.
